I run into sort of same situation, but I think I found better solution using custom plugin when this also works with activation on multiple places such as hover on legend etc.

I have also implemented the hover on legend effect which just set activeElement base on index on which is beforeDatasetsDraw checking.

This is just for pie/doughnut charts with one dataset, to use it for multiple just run loop trough datasets as well.

Since the plugin uses chart.update method, be careful, it can get stuck on recursion, that is also the reason why are there the return branches.

With this solution you don't have to update your chart configuration such as disabling animation or make any other listeners to events.

Don't forget to register plugin to chart instance.

// the color of the other pieces (not hovered)
const fadeColor = 'rgba(76, 76, 76, 0.62)';

const ChartPieceHighlightPlugin: Plugin<'pie'> = {
  id: 'pieceHighlightPlugin',
  afterInit: (chart: Chart<'pie'>, _args, _options) => {
    // init color store
    chart.data.datasets[0].originalBackgroundColor = chart.data.datasets[0].backgroundColor as Array<string>;
  },
  beforeDatasetsDraw: (chart: Chart<'pie'>, _args, _options) => {
    // check if any data in dataset is active (hovered) and fade other pieces
    const activeElements = chart.getActiveElements();
    const dataset = chart.data.datasets[0];

    const someFaded = (dataset.backgroundColor as Array<string>).includes(fadeColor);

    // inactive
    if (!activeElements.length) {
      // default state - no piece highlighted
      if (!someFaded) return;
      // reset colors to original
      dataset.backgroundColor = dataset.originalBackgroundColor;
      chart.update();
      return;
    }

    const activeIndex = activeElements[0].index;
    // active piece first time or if the hover changed to another piece
    if (!someFaded || dataset.backgroundColor[activeIndex] !== dataset.originalBackgroundColor[activeIndex]) {
      // fade all pieces except the active one
      dataset.backgroundColor = dataset.data.map(() => fadeColor);
      (dataset.backgroundColor as Array<string>)[activeIndex] = dataset.originalBackgroundColor[activeIndex];

      chart.update();
      return;
    }
  },
};

export default ChartPieceHighlightPlugin;

My team is also using shared records for avdl based schema definitions. I have two different solutions, both of them are working with avdl schema (not protocol) definitions.

Shared records in one file

Lets say that I have the SharedRecords.avdl file:

namespace public.schemas;

record FirstSharedRecord {
    string? name;
    array<int> someArray;
}

record SecondSharedRecord {
    @logicalType("timestamp-nanos")
    long someNonAvdlSupportedLogicalTypeField;
    decimal(10, 2) decimalField;
}

Then you could import it into your schema by the file name and use the namespace to qualify the named types:

namespace my.schema;
schema MyRecord;
import idl "SharedRecords.avdl";

enum ExampleEnum {
    ONE,
    TWO,
    THREE
} = THREE;

record MyRecord {
    string name;
    ExampleEnum enumField;
    public.schemas.FirstSharedRecord sharedRecordField;
}

Then if you copy the two files to the same destination, you could call the java -jar avro-tools.jar idl MySchema.avdl MySchema.avsc to convert the avdl to avsc. This will result the following avsc:

{
  "type" : "record",
  "name" : "MyRecord",
  "namespace" : "my.schema",
  "fields" : [ {
    "name" : "name",
    "type" : "string"
  }, {
    "name" : "enumField",
    "type" : {
      "type" : "enum",
      "name" : "ExampleEnum",
      "symbols" : [ "ONE", "TWO", "THREE" ],
      "default" : "THREE"
    }
  }, {
    "name" : "sharedRecordField",
    "type" : {
      "type" : "record",
      "name" : "FirstSharedRecord",
      "namespace" : "public.schemas",
      "fields" : [ {
        "name" : "name",
        "type" : [ "null", "string" ]
      }, {
        "name" : "someArray",
        "type" : {
          "type" : "array",
          "items" : "int"
        }
      } ]
    }
  } ]
}

As you can see the SharedRecords schema does not have a schema <record name> line, so technically you can not convert it to an avsc but still you can import it and the final avsc will contain only the records used from that schema.
The other solution (which is not that general) is to define an own avdl file for each shared record/other named schema, then import the ones your concrete schema is using.

These solutions will contain the snapshot of the imported records at the generation time, so they are not updating as you are updating the shared schema file, but I think confluent schema registry doesnt supporting that aswell - how'd you version your schema otherwise?

Note that avro idl schemas are relatievely new (I think it was added with avro 1.12), so protocols were used before which were not suitable for clean schema generation.
